title: Trump tells US allies to 'go to the strait and just take it, protect it' | ABC NEWS
description: US President Donald Trump said allies should take the Strait of Hormuz for themselves and reiterated the US will cease military involvement in Iran within a few weeks. During his first live address since the start of the war, he said US forces would bring Iran "back into the Stone Ages where they belong", including striking the country's energy infrastructure "if there is no deal". Ahead of the address some expected that Trump might have a significant announcement: maybe a ceasefire, maybe a ground invasion, or maybe, based on his statements about NATO, an announcement relating to that pact.  📸 via ABC News by North America correspondent Rachel Clayton. title: Calls for an immediate wartime windfall tax on Australia's gas exporters | The Business | ABC NEWS
description: There are calls for the government to impose a windfall tax on Australia's gas exporters who are making high profits as gas prices soar because of the Middle East conflict.  A Select Committee on the Taxation of Gas Resources is currently looking at the issue and a 25 per cent tax has been floated. Independent MP Allegra Spender is one voice backing a windfall tax on fossil fuel exports. She says Australians should benefit "because when it's a war driven price spike it should be shared with the Australian people." In response to gas company claims it will drive away investors, she says the tax would be structured to provide certainty to business,  as well as capture the benefit of windfall profits. She says no gas company made their investment decision "based on the kind of prices we're seeing right now because of the war." She says this is an opportunity to reform the tax-take for the short and long term. "I think we should reset our structure for taxation of resources both for the resources we do now but also making sure as we develop critical minerals so that we do have the right tax settings for Australians." Allegra Spender says the government needs to make a move on this tax reform now, and not wait until the Federal Budget in May. #ABCBusiness  Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: Global fuel crisis 'will not be like COVID' PM says | ABC NEWS
description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has assured Australians the current fuel crisis "will not be like COVID".   The PM addressed the National Press Club in Canberra today, where he outlined the government's economic priorities ahead of next month's federal budget, and responded to a landmark report into the harms of online gambling.  Last night, he gave a national address, urging Australians to "go about your business and your life as normal" but switch to using public transport if possible, which the opposition has criticised as lacking substance and clarity.  Opposition leader Angus Taylor will exercise his right of reply and deliver a message via the ABC this evening. title: How high will the price of oil go? | The Business | ABC NEWS
description: US President Donald Trump may have declared victory, but markets aren't paying attention. Oil prices surged and stocks sank after his address to the nation, raising questions over where the oil ceiling may be. CBA Head of International Economics, Joe Capurso, says the oil price could exceed $150 a barrel in a worst case scenario.  He says "the war has three participants, and if the Iranians and the Israelis keep going, then the war will keep going ... and what matters most for the world economy is the Strait of Hormuz and whether oil and gas is able to pass through it."  Compared to previous crises, the market reaction has been muted.  Oil prices are well below the peak following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, and adjusted for inflation, it's nowhere near the spike after America's attack on Libya more than a decade ago.  Barely a handful of tankers have transited the Strait of Hormuz in the past month. With so many vessels trapped in the Persian Gulf, the transport shortage has seen shipping prices surge. Shipping oil out of the US is now three times more expensive than a month ago. Add to that processing costs.  Refiners have trebled profit margins on petrol in the past month and quadrupled jet fuel.  Even if the war ended tomorrow, it would take months and possibly years to return to normal.   #ABCBusiness Subscribe: http://ab.co/1svxLVE  ABC NEWS provides around the clock coverage of news events as they break in Australia and abroad. title: State and territory leaders agree to fund further fuel excise cut | ABC NEWS
description: The state and territory leaders have agreed to fund a further cut to the fuel excise of 5.7 cents per litre, bringing the total reduction to about 32 cents per litre until June 30. Anthony Albanese announced the deal while making his strongest criticism yet of the war in the Middle East at National Press Club, and Opposition leader Angus Taylor made his response to the prime minister's national address. Meanwhile, many Australians have been preparing for their Easter break, but rising costs have some feeling uneasy about travel.
